unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! This unit takes you into the great outdoors. You will explore topics connected to outdoor adventures, nature and environmental protection. Whether it is hiking through a forest, camping beside a river, or working together to protect the natural world, this unit gives you the language skills to describe, discuss and write about these experiences with confidence.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

readings.title: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 5! This unit takes you into the great outdoors. You will explore topics connected to outdoor adventures, nature and environmental protection. Whether it is hiking through a forest, camping beside a river, or working together to protect the natural world, this unit gives you the language skills to describe, discuss and write about these experiences with confidence.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use vocabulary related to outdoor equipment, adventure sports, nature features and eco-friendly practices.
Read and understand texts about outdoor activities and environmental challenges.
Use -ing forms (gerunds and present participles) correctly as subjects, objects and after prepositions.
Understand and use the present passive and past passive to describe processes and events.
Form sentences with modal verbs + passive (e.g. "Trees must be protected").
Talk about the future using will, going to and the present continuous for future arrangements.
Compare things using comparative and superlative adjectives.
Write a trip guide or an environmental protection speech.
Speak confidently about outdoor experiences and environmental issues.
Listen to descriptions of adventures and identify key details.
Before you begin reading, look at these important words. You will meet them throughout the unit. Full definitions, examples and practice activities are in your separate Vocabulary file.
expedition — a long, organised journey for a special purpose  |  terrain — the physical features of an area of land  |  summit — the highest point of a mountain  |  campsite — a place where people set up tents to stay outdoors  |  trail — a path through the countryside or wilderness  |  navigate — to find your way using a map or compass  |  survival — the state of continuing to live, especially in difficult conditions
conservation — the protection of natural things such as animals, plants and forests  |  pollution — damage caused to water, air or land by harmful substances  |  ecosystem — all the living things in an area and how they affect each other  |  renewable — a resource that can be replaced naturally and is not used up  |  biodiversity — the variety of plant and animal life in a particular area  |  sustainability — using resources in a way that does not damage the environment for the future  |  deforestation — cutting down or clearing large areas of trees
Unit Vocabulary from Your Word List
You will also practise these important academic words: acknowledge, alleviate, collaborate, demonstrate, establish, evaluate, incorporate, mitigate, resolve, sustain, and many more. Open your Vocabulary file to study the full list.
Read the following passage carefully. It tells the story of a group of students in East Africa who take part in an outdoor expedition that changes the way they think about nature. As you read, underline any words from your Key Vocabulary list.
On a cool Saturday morning in March, a group of Year 7 students from Madarasatul Musa Academy in Garissa gathered at the school gate. They were about to begin a two-day expedition to the Boni National Reserve, one of Kenya’s least-visited protected forests. Their teacher, Ustadh Isaac, had been planning the trip for months.
“Today,” he announced, adjusting his backpack, “we are not just visitors. We are guardians. Everything we see, we must protect.”

readings.title: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The journey took three hours by bus. When the students finally stepped onto the forest trail, the air felt different — cooler, damper, and full of unfamiliar sounds. Tall trees stretched upward, their branches forming a thick canopy that blocked most of the sunlight. Birds called from hidden perches, and somewhere in the distance, a stream gurgled over rocks.
Samira, one of the keenest hikers, was the first to notice the damage. Along the edge of the trail, several large trees had been cut down. Their stumps were pale and raw, surrounded by scattered branches. “Deforestation,” she whispered to her friend Ishmael. “Someone has been logging here illegally.”
Ustadh Isaac gathered the group. “This is exactly what I wanted you to see,” he said seriously. “The Boni Forest is home to rare animals and hundreds of plant species. When trees are removed, the entire ecosystem suffers. Birds lose their nests. The soil washes away in the rain. Rivers become polluted with mud. Biodiversity decreases.”
He asked the students to take notes and photographs to document what they observed. They would use this evidence later to create environmental awareness materials for their school and community.
That afternoon, a local ranger named Ishmael joined the group. He explained how conservation efforts were helping the forest recover. “We have established a tree-planting programme,” he said. “Every month, volunteers plant two hundred seedlings. Protecting the forest is not one person’s job — it requires collaboration.”
The students set up their campsite near a clearing. They collected firewood that had already fallen — they did not break any living branches. They filtered water from the stream and cooked a simple meal of rice, beans and chapati. As the sun set, the sky turned from gold to deep violet, and the first stars appeared. Ishmael pointed upward. “SubhanAllah,” he said quietly. “You cannot see stars like this in the city.”
Around the campfire, Ustadh Isaac asked each student to share one thing they had learned. “I learned that protecting nature is not just about big organisations,” said Samira. “It starts with us — with small choices, like not littering, like planting a tree, like speaking up when something is wrong.”
The next morning, the group joined the ranger’s team to plant thirty saplings along the damaged section of the trail. Each student dug a hole, placed a seedling carefully inside, and covered the roots with soil. It was hard, rewarding work.
On the bus home, the students were tired but changed. They had seen the beauty of the natural world and the threats it faces. They understood that sustainability is not just a word in a textbook — it is a responsibility. And they were ready to become the guardians their teacher had asked them to be.
Answer these questions in full sentences:
According to Ustadh Isaac, what happens to an ecosystem when trees are removed? Give at least three effects.
In your own words, explain what the title "Guardians of the Green" means.
Find each of these words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what it means in your own words:
These questions ask you to go beyond the text and share your own ideas:
The -ing form of a verb is one of the most versatile tools in English. In this section you will learn three important ways to use -ing forms. Detailed lessons with more examples and practice are in your separate Grammar file.

readings.title: Before You Read, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Think about these questions before you begin the story:
As you read, look out for these vocabulary words from your Unit 5 word list. They appear naturally in the story:
abdicate, acknowledge, admonish, alleviate, collaborate, significant, demonstrate, establish, evaluate, embellish, forsake, imitate, incorporate, infringe, plagiarize, inform, relinquish, resolve, sustain, tarnish
The air at Kasisira Secondary School was dry and warm on the morning the student prefect elections were announced. Posters flapped on walls, dust blew over the netball court, and excited whispers filled the corridor. Among the crowd was sixteen-year-old Ibrahim, tall and calm, with a sharp mind and quiet confidence.
Ishmael had never planned to lead. But when his classmates began asking him to run for Head Prefect, he could not help but consider it.
"People respect you," said Nadia, his friend. "You listen. You don't show off. You're fair."
At first, Ibrahim hesitated. Leadership meant responsibility. And mistakes.
That night, he lay awake evaluating the consequences. Becoming Head Prefect would mean sacrificing some free time, facing criticism, and being held to a higher standard. But it would also be a chance to help fix real problems — like the broken water tank or the crumbling library roof.
The next morning, he signed the nomination form. His campaign slogan was simple: Integrity is action.
His main opponent was Bako, a clever boy who was known for charm and style. Bako's speeches were full of energy. But some students whispered that Bako had once been caught trying to plagiarize an essay from the internet.
"I hope he's changed," Ibrahim said.
The week before voting, candidates had to give a speech. Ibrahim stood on the assembly platform and spoke clearly.
"We have problems," he said. "Water is scarce. Classrooms are crowded. Our voices must be heard. I will not promise perfection, but I will promise effort. I will listen, act, and always acknowledge when I am wrong."
The students clapped — not loudly, but sincerely.
Two days later, the results were announced: Ibrahim had won. The head teacher handed him a badge, and the whole school cheered.
His leadership began well. He organised a clean-up day. He met with teachers about better exam preparation. He even worked to establish a suggestion box system.
But a month later, trouble came.
During break, Nadia ran to him, breathless. "Ibrahim — someone has stolen the lab projector."
The projector, donated by a local NGO, was one of the school's few digital tools. Without it, the science department would suffer.
The news spread fast. The head teacher was furious. "This is a significant loss," she said. "The police have been informed."
One teacher suggested, "Security needs to be stricter. Clearly, rules are being infringed."
Investigating the theft became Ibrahim's top priority. He interviewed students, examined the storage room, and reviewed the gate logs. The cameras did not work — that system had never been repaired.
By Friday, suspicion turned toward a group of Form 4 boys who were often late and once found smoking behind the kitchen. Among them was a boy named Job, a known trickster.
Job was called in. He denied everything.
But that night, Ibrahim received an anonymous note under his dorm pillow:
"The projector was hidden. Behind the old library."
He raced there with two friends. Behind the crumbling shelves, wrapped in a sack, was the missing device. Still working. Not stolen — just moved.

readings.title: Before You Read, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
A week later, a Form 3 boy came forward and confessed: "We borrowed it. To practise PowerPoint. For a regional ICT competition."
Ishmael was shocked. "Why not ask?"
The boy shrugged. "No one listens. We thought we'd be admonished. So we hid it."
Ishmael knew the school would want to punish them. But he also knew their intention was not to harm.
He wrote a report to the head teacher. In it, he did not try to embellish the facts. He explained everything honestly and suggested a solution: "The boys should apologise publicly, and they must fix what they've broken — but their motive must be considered."
The head teacher nodded slowly. "This is a reasonable proposal. Well evaluated."
The boys were not expelled. Instead, they helped repair the lab room and wrote a guide for using the projector.
Later, Ibrahim told Nadia, "Sometimes, to lead is to alleviate conflict, not increase it."
But the story did not end there.
Two weeks before the school's Independence Day event, Ibrahim faced another challenge. The student drama group wanted to perform a play that had been written by a famous Ugandan playwright. But one teacher raised a concern: "We must request permission. This play is copyrighted. Using it without permission is plagiarizing."
Some students were angry. "Who will know?" one asked. "Let's just imitate it."
Ishmael shook his head. "We must not pretend. Copying must be avoided. Integrity is not just for show."
Instead, he suggested the students write their own play — about school life. He offered to help them collaborate.
Writing it was not easy. Ideas clashed. Tempers rose. Some students nearly quit. But eventually, a script was developed: funny, honest, and original.
During the performance, laughter echoed in the hall. Teachers smiled. Parents clapped. And when the principal stood to speak, she said, "This is one of the most significant student efforts I have seen."
A local education officer, who had attended, requested a copy to share with other schools.
After the event, Ibrahim reflected. Leading a school was like trying to sustain a flame in the wind. Not flashy, but steady.
Still, challenges continued. During exam week, two students were caught trying to sneak answer sheets.
"They must be suspended," said one teacher. "Cheating must be punished."
But Ibrahim asked, "What led to this?"
After speaking with the students, he learned both were under pressure. One cared for three siblings. The other worked nights to help at his uncle's shop.
"It doesn't excuse them," Ibrahim said, "but understanding the cause can help us respond better."
He recommended that, alongside punishment, the boys be offered support. A reading club was started. Evening prep hours were extended. Discipline was balanced with help.
Certainly, not everyone agreed. Some called Ibrahim naive.
But others began to follow his example.
One teacher said, "Ibrahim reminds us that solving a problem is harder than punishing it — but far more valuable."
Another challenge came when the school's debate club invited students to argue whether leaders should be allowed to relinquish power before their term ends.
He wrote in his diary: "Is knowing when to lead as important as knowing when to leave?"
The next week, he gathered the prefects.
"I believe it's time," he said. "I will abdicate my role early so that a new leader can take over — with fresh ideas."

readings.title: Before You Read, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Ibrahim smiled. "Because true leadership is about growth — not power."
His decision was not forced. It was respected.
His replacement was a younger student, attentive and eager.
At the final assembly, the principal spoke.
"Ibrahim has demonstrated the kind of leadership that teaches others to lead. His actions will not be forgotten. He leaves behind not just policies — but a culture."
As Ibrahim walked off stage, badge in hand, he felt no regret. Only peace.
His time had ended, but the values he planted would remain — like seeds, waiting for the next rain.

grammar.title: -ing Forms (Gerunds and Participles)
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The -ing form of a verb is one of the most flexible tools in English. When we add -ing to a verb, the word can work in different ways: as a noun (called a gerund), as part of a continuous tense, or as a describing word (called a present participle). In this lesson, we focus on using -ing forms as nouns (gerunds) — as the subject of a sentence, as the object after certain verbs, and after prepositions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The -ing form of a verb is one of the most flexible tools in English. When we add -ing to a verb, the word can work in different ways: as a noun (called a gerund), as part of a continuous tense, or as a describing word (called a present participle). In this lesson, we focus on using -ing forms as nouns (gerunds) — as the subject of a sentence, as the object after certain verbs, and after prepositions.
How to form it
Start with the base verb and add -ing:
Most verbs: add -ing directly — walk → walking, read → reading, play → playing
Verbs ending in silent -e: drop the e, then add -ing — hike → hiking, make → making, explore → exploring
Short verbs (CVC pattern): double the final consonant — run → running, swim → swimming, plan → planning
Verbs ending in -ie: change -ie to -y, then add -ing — die → dying, lie → lying
Three ways to use -ing forms
1. As the subject of a sentence (gerund as subject)
The -ing form acts like a noun and sits at the beginning of the sentence:
Hiking is a great way to explore nature.
Recycling helps reduce waste in our communities.
Protecting endangered animals requires effort from everyone.
Swimming in the river was the best part of the trip.
2. As the object after certain verbs (gerund as object)
Some verbs are always followed by the -ing form, not the infinitive (to + verb). Common verbs include: enjoy, avoid, finish, suggest, recommend, keep, practise, consider, mind, imagine, risk, miss, deny.
I enjoy exploring new places.
We should avoid wasting water.
She finished writing her report on deforestation.
He suggested planting trees in the schoolyard.
They kept walking even when the path got difficult.
Can you imagine living without clean water?
3. After prepositions
When a verb follows a preposition (in, on, at, by, about, without, before, after, for, of, instead of), it must be in the -ing form:
She is interested in learning about wildlife.
They succeeded by working as a team.
We talked about organising a clean-up day.
Before leaving, we checked for litter.
Instead of complaining, he offered a solution.
Thank you for helping us plant the trees.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using the infinitive after a preposition.
Wrong: "She is interested in to learn."  Correct: "She is interested in learning."
Mistake: Using the infinitive after verbs that need -ing.
Wrong: "I enjoy to read."  Correct: "I enjoy reading."
Mistake: Confusing a gerund (noun) with the present continuous (tense).
Gerund (noun): "Swimming is fun." (Swimming answers "What is fun?")
Present continuous (tense): "I am swimming right now." (Describes an action happening now.)
Memory tip
Think of gerunds as "action nouns". If you can replace the -ing word with "it" or "something", it is a gerund. Example: "Camping is fun" → "It is fun" — so "camping" is a gerund.
Practice 1
A. Fill in each gap with the -ing form of the verb in brackets:
______ (plant) trees helps clean the air.
I enjoy ______ (camp) in the mountains.
She is afraid of ______ (climb) steep hills.
______ (protect) the environment is everyone's duty.
They avoided ______ (pollute) the river.
After ______ (walk) for two hours, we rested.
He suggested ______ (use) solar panels at school.
______ (reduce) waste starts with small daily choices.
Answers: 1. Planting  2. camping  3. climbing  4. Protecting  5. polluting  6. walking  7. using  8. Reducing
B. Write three sentences of your own using -ing forms: one as a subject, one as an object after a verb, and one after a preposition.

grammar.title: Present Passive
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The present passive voice is used when we want to focus on the action or the thing that receives the action, rather than on who does it. It is especially useful in scientific and environmental writing, where the process matters more than the person.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The present passive voice is used when we want to focus on the action or the thing that receives the action, rather than on who does it. It is especially useful in scientific and environmental writing, where the process matters more than the person.
How to form it
Subject + is/am/are + past participle (+ by + agent)
"The forest is protected by law."
"Trees are planted every spring."
"Plastic is recycled at this facility."
"The water is tested for pollution regularly."
The agent ("by law", "by volunteers") can be left out when it is unknown or unimportant: "The water is tested regularly."
Active vs. passive comparison
Active: Rangers protect the forest. → Passive: The forest is protected by rangers.
Active: Volunteers plant trees every year. → Passive: Trees are planted every year.
Active: Factories pollute the river. → Passive: The river is polluted by factories.
Common mistakes
Mistake: Wrong form of "to be" — "The trees is planted."
Fix: Match the verb to the subject — "The trees are planted." (Trees = plural = are)
Mistake: Forgetting the past participle — "The car is drive."
Fix: Use the past participle — "The car is driven."
Practice 2
Rewrite each active sentence in the present passive:
Scientists study endangered species.
The school recycles paper and plastic.
People use solar energy to power homes.
Rangers monitor the wildlife in the park.
Farmers grow organic vegetables without chemicals.
Answers: 1. Endangered species are studied by scientists.  2. Paper and plastic are recycled by the school.  3. Solar energy is used to power homes.  4. The wildlife in the park is monitored by rangers.  5. Organic vegetables are grown without chemicals.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply present passive in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Past Passive
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The past passive is used to talk about actions that were done to someone or something in the past. It is very useful for reporting events, describing history, and writing about environmental changes that have already happened.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The past passive is used to talk about actions that were done to someone or something in the past. It is very useful for reporting events, describing history, and writing about environmental changes that have already happened.
How to form it
Subject + was/were + past participle (+ by + agent)
"The national park was established in 1985."
"Two hundred trees were planted by volunteers last month."
"The river was polluted by factory waste."
"The damaged trail was repaired before the rainy season."
Use was for singular subjects (he, she, it, the park) and were for plural subjects (they, we, the trees).
Active vs. passive comparison
Active: The storm destroyed the bridge. → Passive: The bridge was destroyed by the storm.
Active: Volunteers cleaned the beach. → Passive: The beach was cleaned by volunteers.
Common mistakes
Mistake: Mixing up was and were — "The trees was planted."
Fix: The trees = plural = were: "The trees were planted."
Practice 3
Rewrite each active sentence in the past passive:
A local farmer discovered the rare bird.
Heavy rains flooded the village last year.
The government built a new wildlife sanctuary.
Illegal loggers destroyed hundreds of trees.
Students organised an environmental awareness day.
Answers: 1. The rare bird was discovered by a local farmer.  2. The village was flooded by heavy rains last year.  3. A new wildlife sanctuary was built by the government.  4. Hundreds of trees were destroyed by illegal loggers.  5. An environmental awareness day was organised by students.

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.

grammar.title: Modal Verbs + Passive
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
When we combine modal verbs (must, should, can, may, might, could) with the passive voice, we can talk about rules, obligations, possibilities and recommendations without naming who does the action. This is very common in environmental discussions and formal writing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
When we combine modal verbs (must, should, can, may, might, could) with the passive voice, we can talk about rules, obligations, possibilities and recommendations without naming who does the action. This is very common in environmental discussions and formal writing.
How to form it
Subject + modal verb + be + past participle
Obligation: "Endangered species must be protected by law."
Advice: "More trees should be planted in urban areas."
Possibility: "Renewable energy can be used to power entire cities."
Permission: "The park may be visited between 8 a.m. and 6 p.m."
Negative: "Plastic bags must not be thrown into the ocean."
Common mistakes
Mistake: Forgetting "be" — "Pollution must stopped."
Fix: Always include "be" — "Pollution must be stopped."
Mistake: Wrong word order — "The forest is must be protected."
Fix: Remove the extra "is" — "The forest must be protected."
Memory tip
The formula is always: Modal + be + done. Think of it as a three-word chain: must-be-protected, should-be-reduced, can-be-achieved.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence using the modal verb in brackets and the passive voice:
Water pollution ______ (must / reduce) immediately.
More national parks ______ (should / establish) to protect wildlife.
Plastic waste ______ (can / recycle) into useful products.
Forests ______ (must / not / destroy) for short-term profit.
Environmental awareness ______ (should / teach) in every school.
Clean energy solutions ______ (can / develop) with enough research.
Answers: 1. must be reduced  2. should be established  3. can be recycled  4. must not be destroyed  5. should be taught  6. can be developed

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.

grammar.title: Expressing the Future
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
English has three main ways to talk about the future. Each one has a slightly different meaning. Choosing the right form helps you communicate clearly whether you are making a prediction, stating a plan, or describing a fixed arrangement.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
English has three main ways to talk about the future. Each one has a slightly different meaning. Choosing the right form helps you communicate clearly whether you are making a prediction, stating a plan, or describing a fixed arrangement.
1. Will — predictions, promises and spontaneous decisions
Structure: Subject + will + base verb
Prediction: "I think it will rain this afternoon."
Promise: "I will help you carry the camping equipment."
Spontaneous decision: "It's getting cold. I will put on my jacket."
Negative: "Don't worry, it won't take long to reach the summit."
2. Going to — plans and evidence-based predictions
Structure: Subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb
Plan: "We are going to visit the Boni Forest next Saturday."
Intention: "I am going to write a report on deforestation."
Evidence: "Look at the sky — it is going to rain soon."
3. Present continuous — fixed future arrangements
Structure: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing (with a future time word)
"We are leaving at 6 a.m. tomorrow." (already arranged)
"The conservation team is meeting on Friday morning."
"I am volunteering at the tree-planting event this weekend."
How to choose the right form
Ask yourself:
Is it a prediction or sudden decision? Use will.
Is it a plan or intention you have already decided? Use going to.
Is it a fixed arrangement with a specific time/place? Use present continuous.
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using "will" for a plan that is already decided.
Wrong: "I will visit the park tomorrow." (if already planned)
Correct: "I am going to visit the park tomorrow."
Mistake: Forgetting am/is/are in "going to" sentences.
Wrong: "She going to help."  Correct: "She is going to help."
Practice 5
Choose the best future form for each sentence:
The eco-club ______ (meet) at the school gate at 7 a.m. on Saturday. (fixed arrangement)
I think solar energy ______ (replace) fossil fuels one day. (general prediction)
We have decided — we ______ (organise) a beach clean-up next month. (plan already decided)
That tree looks unstable. It ______ (fall) in the next storm. (evidence-based prediction)
The rain has stopped! I ______ (go) outside for a walk. (spontaneous decision)
They ______ (fly) to Nairobi on Tuesday for the conference. (fixed arrangement)
Answers: 1. is meeting / are meeting  2. will replace  3. are going to organise  4. is going to fall  5. will go  6. are flying

grammar.title: Comparative and Superlative Forms
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Comparatives and superlatives help us compare people, places and things. This is essential when discussing environmental topics — for example, comparing which energy source is cleaner, which forest is the largest, or which approach is the most effective.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Comparatives and superlatives help us compare people, places and things. This is essential when discussing environmental topics — for example, comparing which energy source is cleaner, which forest is the largest, or which approach is the most effective.
How to form comparatives (comparing two things)
One-syllable adjectives: add -er — tall → taller, clean → cleaner
One-syllable ending in -e: add -r — large → larger, safe → safer
One-syllable CVC: double final consonant + -er — hot → hotter, big → bigger
Two syllables ending in -y: change -y to -i + -er — happy → happier, easy → easier
Two or more syllables: use more — beautiful → more beautiful, sustainable → more sustainable
How to form superlatives (comparing three or more things)
One-syllable: add -est — tall → tallest, clean → cleanest
Two syllables ending in -y: change -y to -i + -est — happy → happiest
Two or more syllables: use most — beautiful → most beautiful
Irregular forms
good → better → best  |  bad → worse → worst  |  far → farther/further → farthest/furthest  |  little → less → least  |  much/many → more → most
Example sentences
Solar energy is cleaner than coal. (comparative)
This is the tallest tree in the forest. (superlative)
Hiking is more enjoyable than sitting indoors. (comparative)
That was the most exciting expedition we have ever been on. (superlative)
This solution is better than the one we tried before. (irregular comparative)
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using "more" with a short adjective — "more tall".
Fix: Use the -er form — "taller."
Mistake: Mixing forms — "more easier".
Fix: Use one form only — "easier."
Practice 6
Write the correct comparative or superlative form:
The Amazon is the ______ (large) rainforest in the world.
Wind power is ______ (efficient) than many people think.
This path is ______ (steep) than the other one.
Recycling is one of the ______ (easy) ways to help the environment.
Deforestation is ______ (bad) now than it was ten years ago.
This national park is the ______ (beautiful) place I have ever visited.
Walking is ______ (healthy) than driving.
This is the ______ (good) camping trip we have ever had.

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Choose one of the two writing tasks below. Follow the planning steps, write your first draft, revise it, and then share the final version with your AI tutor or teacher.
Option A: Write a Trip Guide
Imagine you are writing a guide for younger students at your school who are about to go on an outdoor expedition. Your guide should help them prepare, stay safe, and enjoy the experience.
What to include:
A title and a short introduction explaining where the trip is going and why it is exciting.
A section on what to pack (equipment, clothing, food and water).
A section on safety tips (staying on the trail, what to do if you get lost, basic first aid).
A section on respecting the environment (leave no trace, do not disturb wildlife, collect litter).
A conclusion encouraging the readers to enjoy and learn from the trip.
Language to use:
Imperatives for instructions: "Pack a waterproof jacket.", "Stay on the marked trail."
Future forms for what will happen: "You will hike through beautiful scenery.", "We are going to set up camp by the river."

quizzes.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'acknowledge' mean?
quizzes.options: A serious effort or attempt to do something. | To accept or admit that something is true, or to recognise that you have received something. | To create or start something that is meant to last. | To show how something works or to prove something clearly.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To accept or admit that something is true, or to recognise that you have received something.
quizzes.explanation: I want to acknowledge that you did a great job on your project.

quizzes.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'collaborate' mean?
quizzes.options: A serious effort or attempt to do something. | To create or start something that is meant to last. | To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is. | To work together with others to achieve a common goal.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To work together with others to achieve a common goal.
quizzes.explanation: The students will collaborate to create posters about protecting the environment.

quizzes.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'demonstrate' mean?
quizzes.options: Important or meaningful; large enough to have an effect. | To create or start something that is meant to last. | To show how something works or to prove something clearly. | To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To show how something works or to prove something clearly.
quizzes.explanation: The ranger will demonstrate how to set up a tent safely.

quizzes.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'endeavour' mean?
quizzes.options: A serious effort or attempt to do something. | Important or meaningful; large enough to have an effect. | To find a solution to a problem, or to make a firm decision. | To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A serious effort or attempt to do something.
quizzes.explanation: Learning to navigate with a compass was a worthwhile endeavour.

quizzes.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'establish' mean?
quizzes.options: Important or meaningful; large enough to have an effect. | To create or start something that is meant to last. | To find a solution to a problem, or to make a firm decision. | To finish something, or to reach a decision after thinking carefully.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To create or start something that is meant to last.

quizzes.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'evaluate' mean?

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: To accept or admit that something is true, or to recognise that you have received something. I want to acknowledge that you did a great job on your project.
To work together with others to achieve a common goal. The students will collaborate to create posters about protecting the environment.
To show how something works or to prove something clearly. The ranger will demonstrate how to set up a tent safely.
A serious effort or attempt to do something. Learning to navigate with a compass was a worthwhile endeavour.
To create or start something that is meant to last. The school wants to establish an environmental club for students.
To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is. Before choosing a campsite, we need to evaluate which location is safest.
Important or meaningful; large enough to have an effect. Losing so many trees was a significant blow to the local ecosystem.
To find a solution to a problem, or to make a firm decision. We need to resolve the issue of plastic waste in our rivers.
To finish something, or to reach a decision after thinking carefully. After examining the evidence, the team concluded that the river was polluted.
To communicate or express a message, feeling or idea. The documentary conveyed the importance of protecting rainforests.
